fact: Home to the Great Buddha of Thailand (Phra Phuttha Maha Nawamin), the tallest Buddha statue in the country and the ninth tallest in the world, located at Wat Muang.
Its full ceremonial name is the longest city name in the world, containing 168 letters and taking several seconds to recite in full.
Known for the Chao Phraya Dam, the first large-scale dam built in Thailand to regulate water flow for the central plains.
Famous for the 'Death Railway' and the Bridge over the River Kwai, constructed during World War II.
Hosts an annual Monkey Buffet Festival where locals serve thousands of kilograms of food to the city's macaque population to show gratitude for bringing in tourists.
Features the Khun Dan Prakan Chon Dam, the longest roller-compacted concrete dam in the world.
Home to Phra Pathommachedi, the world's tallest stupa, standing at approximately 120 meters.
Famous for its Nonthaburi Durian, high-quality fruit that is often referred to as the 'King of Durian' and can fetch extremely high prices.
Known as the 'Lotus City' due to the many lotus ponds that once dotted its landscape.
Hosts 'Phra Nakhon Khiri' (Palace on the Hill), a gorgeous 19th-century royal summer palace with a mix of Thai, Chinese, and European architecture.
Was the capital of the Kingdom of Siam for 417 years and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Thailand's narrowest province, with its thinnest point measuring only about 11 kilometers between the Gulf of Thailand and the Myanmar border.
Famous for its intricate traditional pottery, particularly the 'Dragon Jar' (Ong Mongkorn), which is the province's signature product.
Home to the Ancient City (Muang Boran), one of the world's largest outdoor museums, shaped like the map of Thailand.
A major seafood hub known as the 'Kitchen of the World' for its massive seafood markets and processing industry.
The smallest province in Thailand by area, it is famous for the Maeklong Railway Market where vendors retract stalls as trains pass through.
Home to Wat Phra Phutthabat, a revered 17th-century temple containing a sacred footprint of the Buddha.
Features the Phra Non Chaksi, one of the largest and most beautiful reclining Buddha statues in Thailand.
Associated with the classic Thai literary epic 'Khun Chang Khun Phaen', which is set in this province.
Established in 1296 as the capital of the Lanna Kingdom, its name literally means 'New City'.
Home to the world-famous 'White Temple' (Wat Rong Khun), a unique contemporary Buddhist temple with intricate white architecture.
Named 'Wall of Diamonds' (Kamphaeng Phet), referring to its once-impregnable defensive walls from the 14th century.
The only province in Thailand that still uses horse-drawn carriages as a traditional form of transport for tourists.
The smallest of the northern provinces, it was originally the capital of the ancient Hariphunchai Kingdom.
Known as the 'City of Three Mists' due to the thick fog that covers its mountainous landscape during the winter and rainy seasons.
Isolated for centuries by high mountains, Nan developed a unique local culture and dialect that remains distinct today.
Features the scenic Phayao Lake (Kwan Phayao), the largest freshwater lake in Northern Thailand.
Known for its beautiful mountainous scenery and cool climate, often called the 'Little Switzerland' of Thailand.
Known as 'The City of Crocodiles', it is the setting for the famous Thai folk legend of Krai Thong and the crocodile king Chalawan.
Home to Phra Phuttha Chinnarat, widely considered to be one of the most beautiful Buddha images in Thailand.
Features 'Phae Mueang Phi' (Ghost City Canyon), a natural forest area with strange mushroom-shaped rock formations.
The first capital of Siam (mid-13th to 14th century); its name means 'Dawn of Happiness'.
Home to the Thi Lo Su Waterfall, the largest and highest waterfall in Thailand, located in the Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary.
Known for the 'Long Lablae' durian, a rare variety of durian that is seedless and highly prized for its mild scent.
Home to the Huai Kha Khaeng Wildlife Sanctuary, which contains the largest intact tropical dry forest in Mainland Southeast Asia.
Features Phra Mongkhon Ming Mueang, an 11-meter tall Buddha statue made of solid brass with gold leaf.
Thailand's youngest and 77th province, officially separated from Nong Khai in 2011.
Built around and upon ancient volcanic sites, including the stunning Phanom Rung Khmer historical park.
Famous for the Siam Tulips (Dok Krachiao) which bloom in bright pink across the fields of Pa Hin Ngam National Park during the rainy season.
One of the richest areas for dinosaur fossils in Southeast Asia, home to the Sirindhorn Dinosaur Museum.
A major economic hub of the Northeast, it is known for its high-quality Mudmee Silk and unique dinosaur discoveries.
Regularly experiences the coldest temperatures in Thailand, with frosts often appearing on its high mountain peaks.
Often called the 'Taxila of Isan' because it is a major regional center for education with numerous universities and institutes.
Features the Phu Pha Thoep National Park, known for its bizarre mushroom-shaped rock formations created by millions of years of erosion.
Located on the banks of the Mekong River, it features the Phra That Phanom, one of the most sacred Buddhist pagodas in Thailand.
Also known as Korat, it is the largest province by area and the gateway to Northeastern Thailand (Isan).
Features ancient dinosaur bone fossils and shells from millions of years ago, housed in a specialized museum.
Famous for the 'Naga Fireballs', an unexplained natural phenomenon where glowing orbs rise from the Mekong River annually.
Named 'One Hundred and One' (Roi Et), though historically it likely referred to 11 satellite cities.
Home to the annual Wax Castle Festival, where intricate miniatures of temples are carved from wax to celebrate the end of Buddhist Lent.
Rich in Khmer ruins, including the stunning Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai which dates back to the 11th century.
Globally famous for its Elephant Roundup festival, reflecting the deep historical and cultural bond between the local Kuy people and elephants.
Famous for its massive Candle Festival where gigantic, intricately carved wax sculptures are paraded through the city.
Home to Ban Chiang, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with archaeological finds of bronze tools and pottery dating over 4,000 years.
Famous for the 'Bun Bang Fai' Rocket Festival, where massive homemade rockets are launched into the sky to ask for seasonal rain.
Home to Wat Saman Rattanaram, which features one of the world's largest pink reclining Ganesha statues.
The 'City of Gems', it is a world-renowned center for gemstone trading and polishing, particularly rubies and sapphires.
Home to Pattaya, the primary tourist hub, and the annual Buffalo Racing Festival held in October.
Known for the Khao Yai National Park (shared with other provinces) and its plentiful fruit orchards, especially pomelo.
A major producer of seafood, fruit, and industrial products; it is the birthplace of the great Thai court poet Sunthorn Phu.
Located on the border with Cambodia, it is home to the massive Rong Kluea Market, one of Thailand's largest second-hand goods markets.
The easternmost mainland province, it serves as the gateway to the Koh Chang archipelago, which consists of 52 islands.
Known as the 'Gateway to the South', its long coastline offers some of Thailand's best snorkeling and diving without the crowds.
Famous for its spectacular limestone karsts and cliffs that attract rock climbers from all over the world.
One of the oldest cities in Thailand, it was historically the center of the Southern Thai kingdom of Tambralinga.
Features the Phra Phuttha Thaksin Ming Mongkhon, a massive seated Buddha statue covered in gold-colored tiles.
Home to the historic Krue Se Mosque, an ancient structure with a mix of architectural styles dating back to the 16th century.
Home to Phang Nga Bay Marine National Park, famous for its limestone karsts including the iconic 'James Bond Island'.
Considered the spiritual home of 'Nora' and 'Nang Talung' (shadow puppetry), two of Southern Thailand's most iconic performing arts.
Thailand's largest island, it was once a major center for tin mining before becoming a premier global tourism destination.
Known for having the highest rainfall in Thailand and for its natural geothermal hot springs that are free for public use.
Home to the Tarutao National Marine Park, the first marine national park in Thailand and once a penal colony for political prisoners.
Home to the largest freshwater lake in Thailand, Songkhla Lake, which supports a rare population of Irrawaddy dolphins.
Known as the 'Province of a Thousand Islands', it is the gateway to Samui, Phangan, and Tao islands.
Famous for its unique Underwater Wedding Ceremony, held annually near Valentine's Day off the coast of Ko Kradan.
The only landlocked province in Southern Thailand, it is home to the stunning Hala-Bala Wildlife Sanctuary.
